# bankruptcymeanstest.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on bankruptcymeanstest.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Chapter 7 Means Test Alabama: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/alabama.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Alabama Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Alabama by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Alabama. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Alabama Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Alabama by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$56,050$4,671

2 persons$72,865$6,072

3 persons$85,196$7,100

4 persons$99,208$8,267

5 persons$110,308$9,192

6 people$121,408$10,117

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Alabama

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Alabama requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Alabama state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

Alabama has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Alabama Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $56,050, Alabama has one of the lower income thresholds nationally. Filers whose income is close to this amount should carefully calculate their 6-month average, as even small fluctuations can determine whether they pass Part 1. Timing your filing date strategically can make a difference.

Tips for Filing in Alabama

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Alabama's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Alabama's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Alabama?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Alabama, the current median is approximately $56,050 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $99,208. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Alabama bankruptcy?

Alabama requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Alabama?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

## Chapter 7 Means Test Alaska: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/alaska.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Alaska Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Alaska by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Alaska. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Alaska Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Alaska by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,866$6,156

2 persons$96,026$8,002

3 persons$112,276$9,356

4 persons$130,743$10,895

5 persons$141,843$11,820

6 people$152,943$12,745

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Alaska

Federal Exemptions

Available

Alaska is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Alaska has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Alaska Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Alaska is $73,866, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Alaska

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Alaska state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Alaska?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Alaska, the current median is approximately $73,866 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $130,743. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Alaska?

Yes. Alaska allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Alaska?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

## Chapter 7 Means Test Arizona: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/arizona.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Arizona Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Arizona by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Arizona. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Arizona Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Arizona by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$65,014$5,418

2 persons$84,518$7,043

3 persons$98,821$8,235

4 persons$115,075$9,590

5 persons$126,175$10,515

6 people$137,275$11,440

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Arizona

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Arizona requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Arizona state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Arizona has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Arizona Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Arizona is $65,014, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Arizona

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Arizona's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Arizona?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Arizona, the current median is approximately $65,014 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $115,075. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Arizona bankruptcy?

Arizona requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Arizona?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Arkansas: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/arkansas.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Arkansas Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Arkansas by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Arkansas. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Arkansas Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Arkansas by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$54,714$4,560

2 persons$71,128$5,927

3 persons$83,165$6,930

4 persons$96,844$8,070

5 persons$107,944$8,995

6 people$119,044$9,920

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Arkansas

Federal Exemptions

Available

Arkansas is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

2 districts

Arkansas has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Arkansas Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $54,714, Arkansas has one of the lower income thresholds nationally. Filers whose income is close to this amount should carefully calculate their 6-month average, as even small fluctuations can determine whether they pass Part 1. Timing your filing date strategically can make a difference.

Tips for Filing in Arkansas

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Arkansas state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Confirm which of Arkansas's 2 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Arkansas?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Arkansas, the current median is approximately $54,714 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $96,844. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Arkansas?

Yes. Arkansas allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Arkansas?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Calculator: Parts 1 and 2 [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/calculator.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Chapter 7 Means Test Calculator

The only free calculator that does both Part 1 and Part 2 of the means test. Updated with April 2026 Census/DOJ median income data and current IRS expense standards.

Important: This calculator performs both Part 1 (income screening) and Part 2 (expense deductions) of the Chapter 7 means test. Median income data is from the Census Bureau / DOJ, effective April 1, 2026. IRS expense standards effective April 21, 2025 (in effect through June 2026). Housing uses state-level averages; the official form uses county-level data. Always verify at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Means Test Income Screening

Three steps. Instant result. No data collected.

1
Select your state

Choose the state where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

-- Choose a state --

2
Household size

Count everyone in your household -- yourself, spouse (even if not filing), and dependents.

-- Select --
1 person
2 people
3 people
4 people
5 people
6 people
7 people
8 people
9 people
10+ people

3
Average gross monthly income

Add up all gross income (before taxes) for the last 6 full calendar months, then divide by 6. Include wages, self-employment, pensions, Social Security, rental income, and any other regular income.

Enter your average monthly gross income. We will annualize it (multiply by 12) to compare against the annual median.

Check If You Qualify

Start Over

Part 2: Expense Deductions

The IRS allows standard deductions that reduce your disposable income. Many above-median filers still qualify.

Pre-filled amounts are IRS National/Local Standards based on your state and household size. Enter your actual amounts for taxes, insurance, and debt payments. All amounts are monthly.

4
IRS Standard Deductions (auto-filled)

These are set by the IRS based on your household size and state. You cannot change them on the official form.

5
Your Actual Expenses

Enter your actual monthly amounts. Leave blank or 0 if not applicable.

Taxes withheld (monthly)

Federal, state, local income taxes + FICA (from pay stub)

Mandatory payroll deductions (monthly)

Union dues, mandatory retirement contributions, uniform costs

Health insurance premiums (monthly)

Your share of premiums for medical, dental, vision (not employer-paid portion)

Term life / disability insurance (monthly)

Number of vehicles with loan/lease payments

0 -- no vehicle payments
1 vehicle
2 vehicles

Total monthly vehicle payments

Combined loan/lease payments (capped at $662/vehicle)

Mortgage / rent payment (monthly)

Total monthly mortgage (including property tax + insurance escrow) or rent

Other secured debt payments (monthly)

Any secured debts not listed above (furniture loans, etc.)

Priority debt payments (monthly)

Past-due taxes, child support arrears, student loans divided by 60 months

Child care / dependent care (monthly)

Education expenses for dependent children (monthly)

K-12 education, capped at $209.58 per child per month

Charitable contributions (monthly)

To qualified organizations, capped at 15% of gross income

Total nonpriority unsecured debt

Credit cards, medical bills, personal loans (total balance, not monthly payment)

Calculate Part 2 Result

How the Chapter 7 Means Test Works

The means test was introduced by BAPCPA in 2005 to determine whether a debtor's income is low enough to file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. It has two parts:

Part 1: Income Screening

Compare your annualized income (average of last 6 months, multiplied by 12) to the median income for your state and household size. If you are below the median, you pass -- no further analysis needed. This calculator performs both parts.

Part 2: Expense Deductions

If your income is above the median, you may still qualify. Part 2 subtracts allowable expenses (IRS standards, actual secured debt payments, priority debts) from your income. If disposable income is low enough, you can still file Chapter 7.

Gather 6 months of income. Add up all gross income from all sources for the 6 full calendar months before your filing date. Divide by 6 to get your average monthly income.

Annualize. Multiply that monthly average by 12. This is your "current monthly income" annualized -- the number the means test uses.

Compare to state median. The DOJ publishes median income figures by state and household size, updated every April and November. If your annualized income is at or below the median, you pass Part 1.

If above median, complete Form 122A-2. Deduct allowed expenses from your income. If your remaining disposable income falls below the statutory threshold, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

File Form 122A-1. Every Chapter 7 filer must file this form, even if they pass Part 1. It documents the calculation for the court.

Who Is Exempt from the Means Test?

Disabled Veterans

Veterans with disabilities incurred during active duty or homeland defense are exempt from the means test entirely, under 11 U.S.C. Section 707(b)(2)(D).

Non-Consumer Debts

If your debts are primarily business debts rather than consumer debts, the means test does not apply. The 50% threshold is calculated from your total debt.

National Guard / Reserves

Members called to active duty or homeland defense for 90+ days in the preceding 540 days are exempt from the means test.

Income Below Median

If you pass Part 1 (below state median), you effectively pass the entire means test. No expense calculation is needed.

What Counts as Income on the Means Test?

The means test uses "current monthly income" (CMI) as defined in 11 U.S.C. Section 101(10A). This is broader than what you might think of as income:

Included: Wages, salary, tips, bonuses, overtime, commissions

Included: Net business or self-employment income

Included: Rental and real property income

Included: Interest, dividends, royalties

Included: Pension and retirement income

Included: Workers' compensation, disability, unemployment

Included: Alimony, maintenance, support received

Included: Contributions from non-filing spouse or others to household expenses

Excluded: Social Security benefits (the one major exclusion)

Excluded: Payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens if I am above the state median income?

Being above the median does not automatically disqualify you from Chapter 7. Use the Part 2 calculator above to apply IRS-standard expense deductions including housing, transportation, taxes, insurance, and debt payments. Many above-median filers still qualify. If your 60-month disposable income falls below $8,175, there is no presumption of abuse. Chapter 13 remains an option if you do not qualify for Chapter 7.

How far back does the means test look at my income?

The means test uses the average of your gross income over the 6 full calendar months before your filing date. This is called "current monthly income" under 11 U.S.C. Section 101(10A). Note: it uses full calendar months, not the most recent 180 days. If your income has recently dropped (e.g., job loss), you may benefit from waiting to file so the higher-income months roll off the 6-month window.

Do I include my spouse's income on the means test?

If you are married and filing alone (not jointly), you must include your spouse's income on Form 122A-1 -- but you can then deduct a "marital adjustment" for the portion of your spouse's income that is not used for your household expenses or your dependents' expenses. If filing jointly, both incomes are included and compared against the median for your combined household size.

When do the median income figures change?

The DOJ updates the median family income data twice per year, effective for cases filed on or after April 1 and November 1. The data comes from the Census Bureau's American Community Survey. The figures in this calculator are based on the April 2026 data (published March 18, 2026). Always confirm the current figures at justice.gov/ust/means-testing before filing.

What is Form 122A and where do I get it?

Form 122A-1 (Chapter 7 Statement of Your Current Monthly Income) is the official means test form. All Chapter 7 filers must complete it. If your income is above the state median, you must also complete Form 122A-2 (Chapter 7 Means Test Calculation). Both forms are available for free at uscourts.gov. This calculator helps you estimate the result of 122A-1 before you fill out the official form.

Need More Bankruptcy Answers?

Check discharge eligibility, explore the automatic stay, or research your district's statistics.

## Chapter 7 Means Test California: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/california.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

California Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for California by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in California. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

California Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for California by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$79,253$6,604

2 persons$103,029$8,586

3 persons$120,465$10,039

4 persons$140,278$11,690

5 persons$151,378$12,615

6 people$162,478$13,540

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in California

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

California requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by California state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

4 districts

California has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

California Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in California is $79,253, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in California

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review California's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of California's 4 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in California?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in California, the current median is approximately $79,253 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $140,278. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in California bankruptcy?

California requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in California?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

## Chapter 7 Means Test Colorado: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/colorado.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Colorado Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Colorado by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Colorado. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Colorado Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Colorado by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$82,700$6,892

2 persons$107,510$8,959

3 persons$125,704$10,475

4 persons$146,379$12,198

5 persons$157,479$13,123

6 people$168,579$14,048

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Colorado

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Colorado requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Colorado state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Colorado has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Colorado Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $82,700, Colorado has one of the higher income thresholds in the country. This means more filers in Colorado may pass Part 1 of the means test compared to lower-income states. However, the higher cost of living in Colorado often offsets the higher median.

Tips for Filing in Colorado

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Colorado's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Colorado?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Colorado, the current median is approximately $82,700 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $146,379. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Colorado bankruptcy?

Colorado requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Colorado?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Common Mistakes with Means Test Guide
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/common-mistakes.html

Means Test Guide | Discharge Screener | Research

Common Mistakes with Means Test Guide

Avoid these common errors when dealing with means test guide in bankruptcy. Based on analysis of thousands of federal court records. Updated 2026-03-29.

Mistake #1: Not understanding the timeline

Many debtors fail to understand the specific timing requirements related to means test guide. Missing a deadline can result in case dismissal or loss of rights.

Mistake #2: Incomplete or inaccurate filings

Filing errors related to means test guide are among the most common causes of case delays and dismissals. Double-check all forms and schedules before submission.

Mistake #3: Failing to disclose relevant information

Bankruptcy requires full disclosure. Omitting information related to means test guide -- whether intentional or accidental -- can result in denial of discharge or criminal penalties.

Mistake #4: Not attending required hearings

Missing a court hearing related to means test guide can result in automatic dismissal. Always confirm dates and appear on time.

Mistake #5: Ignoring creditor communications

After filing, some creditors may still contact you about means test guide issues. Ignoring these communications or failing to report stay violations can harm your case.

Mistake #6: Choosing the wrong chapter

The bankruptcy chapter you file under significantly affects how means test guide applies to your case. Filing under the wrong chapter can lead to dismissal or unfavorable outcomes.

Mistake #7: Not seeking professional help when needed

While pro se filing is possible, complex means test guide issues often benefit from professional guidance. The cost of an attorney may be less than the cost of mistakes.

Mistake #8: Waiting too long to file

Timing matters with means test guide. Waiting too long can mean lost opportunities, accumulated fees, or changed eligibility requirements.

How to Protect Yourself

Research before filing -- understand the rules that apply to your situation

Keep meticulous records -- document everything related to your bankruptcy

Meet every deadline -- use a calendar and set reminders

Be completely honest -- full disclosure protects you

Use free tools -- our discharge screener can help you understand your options

Get Free Bankruptcy Information

Check your discharge eligibility with our free screener tool.

Check Eligibility Now

Stay Informed

## Chapter 7 Means Test Connecticut: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/connecticut.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Connecticut Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Connecticut by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Connecticut. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Connecticut Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Connecticut by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$87,411$7,284

2 persons$113,634$9,470

3 persons$132,865$11,072

4 persons$154,717$12,893

5 persons$165,817$13,818

6 people$176,917$14,743

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Connecticut

Federal Exemptions

Available

Connecticut is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Connecticut has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Connecticut Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $87,411, Connecticut has one of the higher income thresholds in the country. This means more filers in Connecticut may pass Part 1 of the means test compared to lower-income states. However, the higher cost of living in Connecticut often offsets the higher median.

Tips for Filing in Connecticut

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Connecticut state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Connecticut?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Connecticut, the current median is approximately $87,411 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $154,717. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Connecticut?

Yes. Connecticut allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Connecticut?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Delaware: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/delaware.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Delaware Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Delaware by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Delaware. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Delaware Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Delaware by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$72,198$6,016

2 persons$93,857$7,821

3 persons$109,741$9,145

4 persons$127,790$10,649

5 persons$138,890$11,574

6 people$149,990$12,499

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Delaware

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Delaware requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Delaware state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Delaware has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Delaware Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Delaware is $72,198, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Delaware

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Delaware's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Delaware?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Delaware, the current median is approximately $72,198 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $127,790. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Delaware bankruptcy?

Delaware requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Delaware?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test District of Columbia: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/district-of-columbia.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

District of Columbia Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for District of Columbia by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in District of Columbia. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

District of Columbia Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for District of Columbia by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$99,636$8,303

2 persons$129,527$10,794

3 persons$151,447$12,621

4 persons$176,356$14,696

5 persons$187,456$15,621

6 people$198,556$16,546

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in District of Columbia

Federal Exemptions

Available

District of Columbia is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

District of Columbia has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

District of Columbia Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $99,636, District of Columbia has one of the higher income thresholds in the country. This means more filers in District of Columbia may pass Part 1 of the means test compared to lower-income states. However, the higher cost of living in District of Columbia often offsets the higher median.

Tips for Filing in District of Columbia

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and District of Columbia state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in District of Columbia?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in District of Columbia, the current median is approximately $99,636 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $176,356. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in District of Columbia?

Yes. District of Columbia allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in District of Columbia?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Means Test Expense Deductions (2026)
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/expense-deductions.html

Skip to main content

bankruptcymeanstest.org
☰

Home
Calculator
FAQ
Mistakes

Deductions

Means Test
Allowable Expense Deductions

IRS Standards, actual payments, and special deductions that can make the difference between qualifying and not.

Types of Deductions

If you're above the state median and must complete Part 2 of the means test, these deductions reduce your disposable income. The lower your disposable income, the more likely you qualify for Chapter 7.

IRS National Standards

These are set amounts based on household size for food, clothing, personal care, and other necessities. You use the IRS amount regardless of your actual spending. For 2026, a single person gets approximately $785/month; family of 4 approximately $1,673/month.

IRS Local Standards -- Housing and Utilities

These vary by county and household size. They cover mortgage/rent and utility costs. You use the IRS amount OR your actual payment, depending on the form instructions. In high-cost areas, these deductions can be substantial.

IRS Local Standards -- Transportation

Ownership costs (car payment or lease) and operating costs (gas, insurance, maintenance) have separate allowances. If you own a car free and clear, you can still deduct operating costs but not ownership costs.

Actual Expense Deductions

Some expenses use actual amounts: mandatory payroll deductions (taxes, Social Security), health insurance premiums, disability insurance, term life insurance, court-ordered payments (child support, restitution), child care, education for employment, and care of elderly or disabled household members.

Secured Debt Payments

Your average monthly payment on secured debts (mortgage, car loan) divided by 60 is deducted. If you pay $1,500/month on your mortgage and $400/month on your car, that's $1,900/month in deductions.

Special Circumstances

If standard deductions don't capture your actual situation, you can claim "special circumstances" that justify additional expenses. Common examples: medical conditions requiring ongoing treatment, necessary educational expenses for a disabled child, or natural disaster expenses. You must document these thoroughly.

Frequently Asked Questions

What expenses can I deduct on the means test?

IRS National Standards (food, clothing), IRS Local Standards (housing, transportation), actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance premiums, child care, and other documented expenses.

Do I use actual expenses or IRS Standards?

It depends on the category. Some expenses use the IRS standard amount regardless of actual spending. Others use actual amounts. The means test form (122A-2) specifies which applies.

What are special circumstances deductions?

Additional expenses not captured by standard categories. You must demonstrate they are reasonable and necessary. Examples: medical treatment, disabled family member care, or extraordinary commuting costs.

Can I increase my deductions to pass the means test?

You should claim all legitimate deductions. However, fabricating or inflating expenses is fraud. An experienced attorney can identify deductions you may have missed.

Related Topics on This Site

How It Works

Income Exclusions

Calculator

## Frequently Asked Questions About Means Test Guide
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/faq.html

Means Test Guide | Discharge Screener | Research

Frequently Asked Questions About Means Test Guide

Common questions about means test guide in bankruptcy, answered clearly and concisely. Last updated 2026-03-29.

What is means test guide?

Means Test Guide is a key concept in bankruptcy law under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b). It affects how debtors, creditors, and the court interact during the bankruptcy process. Understanding means test guide is essential for anyone considering or going through bankruptcy.

Who does means test guide apply to?

Means Test Guide applies to individuals and businesses filing for bankruptcy protection. The specific rules vary depending on the chapter filed (Chapter 7, Chapter 13, or Chapter 11) and the debtor's circumstances.

How does means test guide work in Chapter 7?

In Chapter 7 bankruptcy, means test guide operates within the liquidation framework. The trustee and court apply the relevant rules to determine how means test guide affects the case outcome.

How does means test guide work in Chapter 13?

Chapter 13 cases involve a repayment plan, and means test guide plays a specific role in how that plan is structured, confirmed, and completed over the 3-5 year plan period.

Can means test guide affect my discharge?

Yes. Means Test Guide can directly impact whether you receive a discharge, when you receive it, and what debts are covered. Understanding these rules before filing is critical.

What happens if I violate rules about means test guide?

Violations related to means test guide can result in case dismissal, denial of discharge, sanctions, or other adverse consequences. Courts take compliance seriously.

Do I need a lawyer for means test guide issues?

While not legally required, having an experienced bankruptcy attorney can be valuable when dealing with means test guide issues. Complex situations especially benefit from professional guidance.

How does means test guide differ by state?

While bankruptcy is federal law, means test guide can be affected by state-specific exemptions, local court rules, and district practices. Missouri, Kansas, and other states may have different approaches.

What are common mistakes with means test guide?

Common mistakes include misunderstanding eligibility requirements, missing deadlines, failing to disclose information, and not properly accounting for means test guide in the bankruptcy petition.

Where can I learn more about means test guide?

The Open Bankruptcy Project provides free, research-backed information about means test guide and all aspects of bankruptcy law. Visit our research platform for detailed analysis.

What should I know about form 122A?

Form 122A is an important aspect of means test guide. This involves specific rules and requirements that can affect your bankruptcy case. Consult the relevant statute and local court rules for detailed guidance.

What should I know about income calculation?

Income Calculation is an important aspect of means test guide. This involves specific rules and requirements that can affect your bankruptcy case. Consult the relevant statute and local court rules for detailed guidance.

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Guide

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Florida: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/florida.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Florida Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Florida by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Florida. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Florida Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Florida by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$69,876$5,823

2 persons$90,839$7,570

3 persons$106,212$8,851

4 persons$123,681$10,307

5 persons$134,781$11,232

6 people$145,881$12,157

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Florida

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Florida requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Florida state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

Florida has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Florida Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Florida is $69,876, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Florida

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Florida's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Florida's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Florida?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Florida, the current median is approximately $69,876 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $123,681. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Florida bankruptcy?

Florida requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Florida?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

## Chapter 7 Means Test Georgia: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/georgia.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Georgia Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Georgia by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Georgia. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Georgia Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Georgia by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$65,628$5,469

2 persons$85,316$7,110

3 persons$99,755$8,313

4 persons$116,162$9,680

5 persons$127,262$10,605

6 people$138,362$11,530

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Georgia

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Georgia requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Georgia state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

Georgia has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Georgia Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Georgia is $65,628, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Georgia

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Georgia's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Georgia's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Georgia?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Georgia, the current median is approximately $65,628 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $116,162. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Georgia bankruptcy?

Georgia requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Georgia?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Hawaii: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/hawaii.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Hawaii Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Hawaii by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Hawaii. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Hawaii Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Hawaii by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$86,427$7,202

2 persons$112,355$9,363

3 persons$131,369$10,947

4 persons$152,976$12,748

5 persons$164,076$13,673

6 people$175,176$14,598

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Hawaii

Federal Exemptions

Available

Hawaii is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Hawaii has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Hawaii Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $86,427, Hawaii has one of the higher income thresholds in the country. This means more filers in Hawaii may pass Part 1 of the means test compared to lower-income states. However, the higher cost of living in Hawaii often offsets the higher median.

Tips for Filing in Hawaii

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Hawaii state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Hawaii?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Hawaii, the current median is approximately $86,427 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $152,976. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Hawaii?

Yes. Hawaii allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Hawaii?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

## How the Bankruptcy Means Test Works (2026)
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/how-means-test-works.html

Skip to main content

bankruptcymeanstest.org
☰

Home
Calculator
FAQ
Mistakes

How It Works

How the Bankruptcy
Means Test Works

Step-by-step explanation of the two-part means test with real examples.

The Two-Part Test

The means test has two parts. Part 1 compares your income to the state median. If you're below, you pass -- no further calculation needed. If above, Part 2 determines whether your disposable income is low enough to still qualify for Chapter 7.

About 70% of Chapter 7 filers pass at Part 1 (below median). Of those who proceed to Part 2, many still qualify after expense deductions.

Part 1: Income vs. Median

Calculate your "current monthly income" (CMI): total household gross income over the past 6 full calendar months, divided by 6. Include wages, self-employment, rental income, pensions, unemployment, contributions from household members, and most other sources. Exclude Social Security.

Multiply CMI by 12 to annualize. Compare to your state's median income for your household size. Below median = pass.

Part 2: Disposable Income

If above median, subtract allowed expenses: IRS Local Standards (housing, utilities), IRS National Standards (food, clothing, personal care), transportation (ownership costs, operating costs), actual secured debt payments, priority debt payments, and other authorized deductions.

If monthly disposable income x 60 is less than $8,175, you pass. If more than $13,650, you fail (presumption of abuse). In between, the calculation gets more complex.

Real Example

Jane is a single filer in Missouri. Her 6-month average gross income is $3,800/month ($45,600 annualized). Missouri's 2026 median for a household of 1 is approximately $52,000. Jane is below median -- she passes Part 1 and qualifies for Chapter 7.

Tom is a single filer in Missouri earning $5,000/month ($60,000 annualized) -- above median. After IRS Standards and deductions, his disposable income is $150/month. $150 x 60 = $9,000. This is between $8,175 and $13,650, so further analysis is needed. His attorney determines he still qualifies after additional deductions.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the means test?

The means test determines Chapter 7 eligibility by comparing your income to your state median and calculating your disposable income after allowed expenses.

Do I have to take the means test?

All individual Chapter 7 filers must complete it. Exceptions: disabled veterans meeting certain criteria, and debtors whose debts are primarily business debts.

What if I barely fail the means test?

You can file Chapter 13, wait until your income drops, or explore whether additional deductions were missed. Many people who initially appear above the threshold qualify after proper expense analysis.

Is the means test the only eligibility requirement?

No. You also cannot have received a Chapter 7 discharge within 8 years, must have completed credit counseling, and must not have had a case dismissed for bad faith within 180 days.

Related Topics on This Site

Means Test Calculator

What Income Is Excluded

Allowable Deductions

Last updated: April 2026. Not legal advice.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Idaho: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/idaho.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Idaho Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Idaho by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Idaho. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Idaho Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Idaho by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$65,394$5,450

2 persons$85,012$7,084

3 persons$99,399$8,283

4 persons$115,747$9,646

5 persons$126,847$10,571

6 people$137,947$11,496

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Idaho

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Idaho requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Idaho state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Idaho has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Idaho Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Idaho is $65,394, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Idaho

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Idaho's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Idaho?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Idaho, the current median is approximately $65,394 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $115,747. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Idaho bankruptcy?

Idaho requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Idaho?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.
