## Chapter 7 Means Test Missouri: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/missouri.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Missouri Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Missouri by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Missouri. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Missouri Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Missouri by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$64,972$5,414

2 persons$84,464$7,039

3 persons$98,757$8,230

4 persons$115,000$9,583

5 persons$126,100$10,508

6 people$137,200$11,433

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Missouri

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Missouri requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Missouri state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

2 districts

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Missouri Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Missouri is $64,972, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Missouri

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Missouri's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Missouri's 2 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Missouri?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Missouri, the current median is approximately $64,972 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $115,000. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Missouri bankruptcy?

Missouri requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Missouri?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

1 person$66,501$5,542

2 persons$86,451$7,204

3 persons$101,082$8,424

4 persons$117,707$9,809

5 persons$128,807$10,734

6 people$139,907$11,659

Bankruptcy Filing in Montana

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Montana requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Montana state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Montana has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Montana Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Montana is $66,501, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Montana

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Montana's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Montana?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Montana, the current median is approximately $66,501 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $117,707. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Montana bankruptcy?

Montana requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

2026 median income limits for Nebraska by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Nebraska Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Nebraska by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$71,277$5,940

2 persons$92,660$7,722

3 persons$108,341$9,028

4 persons$126,160$10,513

5 persons$137,260$11,438

6 people$148,360$12,363

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Nebraska

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Nebraska requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Nebraska state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Nebraska has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Nebraska Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Nebraska is $71,277, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Nebraska

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Nebraska's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Nebraska?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Nebraska, the current median is approximately $71,277 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $126,160. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Nebraska requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Nevada: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/nevada.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Nevada Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Nevada by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Nevada. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Nevada Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Nevada by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$68,271$5,689

2 persons$88,752$7,396

3 persons$103,772$8,648

4 persons$120,840$10,070

5 persons$131,940$10,995

6 people$143,040$11,920

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Nevada

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Nevada requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Nevada state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Nevada has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Nevada Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Nevada is $68,271, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Nevada

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Nevada's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Nevada?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Nevada, the current median is approximately $68,271 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $120,840. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Nevada bankruptcy?

Nevada requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Nevada?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

Means Test Guide

What is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

Automatic Stay ·
341 Meeting ·
523(a) Exceptions ·
727(a)(8) ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·
How to File ·
Cost Guide

## Chapter 7 Means Test New Hampshire: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/new-hampshire.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

New Hampshire Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for New Hampshire by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in New Hampshire. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

New Hampshire Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for New Hampshire by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$86,997$7,250

2 persons$113,096$9,425

3 persons$132,235$11,020

4 persons$153,985$12,832

5 persons$165,085$13,757

6 people$176,185$14,682

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in New Hampshire

Federal Exemptions

Available

New Hampshire is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

New Hampshire has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

New Hampshire Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $86,997, New Hampshire has one of the higher income thresholds in the country. This means more filers in New Hampshire may pass Part 1 of the means test compared to lower-income states. However, the higher cost of living in New Hampshire often offsets the higher median.

Tips for Filing in New Hampshire

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and New Hampshire state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in New Hampshire?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in New Hampshire, the current median is approximately $86,997 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $153,985. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in New Hampshire?

Yes. New Hampshire allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in New Hampshire?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

Means Test Guide

What is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

## Chapter 7 Means Test New Jersey: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

New Jersey Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for New Jersey by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in New Jersey. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

New Jersey Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for New Jersey by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$93,051$7,754

2 persons$120,966$10,080

3 persons$141,438$11,786

4 persons$164,700$13,725

5 persons$175,800$14,650

6 people$186,900$15,575

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in New Jersey

Federal Exemptions

Available

New Jersey is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

New Jersey has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

New Jersey Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $93,051, New Jersey has one of the higher income thresholds in the country. This means more filers in New Jersey may pass Part 1 of the means test compared to lower-income states. However, the higher cost of living in New Jersey often offsets the higher median.

Tips for Filing in New Jersey

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and New Jersey state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in New Jersey?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in New Jersey, the current median is approximately $93,051 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $164,700. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in New Jersey?

Yes. New Jersey allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in New Jersey?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

New Mexico Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for New Mexico by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in New Mexico. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

New Mexico Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for New Mexico by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$57,528$4,794

2 persons$74,786$6,232

3 persons$87,443$7,287

4 persons$101,825$8,485

5 persons$112,925$9,410

6 people$124,025$10,335

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in New Mexico

Federal Exemptions

Available

New Mexico is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

New Mexico has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

New Mexico Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $57,528, New Mexico has one of the lower income thresholds nationally. Filers whose income is close to this amount should carefully calculate their 6-month average, as even small fluctuations can determine whether they pass Part 1. Timing your filing date strategically can make a difference.

Tips for Filing in New Mexico

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and New Mexico state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in New Mexico?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in New Mexico, the current median is approximately $57,528 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $101,825. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in New Mexico?

Yes. New Mexico allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in New Mexico?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test New York: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/new-york.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

New York Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for New York by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in New York. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

New York Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for New York by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,272$6,106

2 persons$95,254$7,938

3 persons$111,373$9,281

4 persons$129,691$10,808

5 persons$140,791$11,733

6 people$151,891$12,658

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in New York

Federal Exemptions

Available

New York is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

4 districts

New York has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

New York Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in New York is $73,272, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in New York

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and New York state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Confirm which of New York's 4 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in New York?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in New York, the current median is approximately $73,272 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $129,691. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in New York?

Yes. New York allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in New York?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

2026 median income limits for North Carolina by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in North Carolina. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

North Carolina Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for North Carolina by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,339$5,278

2 persons$82,341$6,862

3 persons$96,275$8,023

4 persons$112,110$9,342

5 persons$123,210$10,268

6 people$134,310$11,192

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in North Carolina

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

North Carolina requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by North Carolina state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

North Carolina has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

North Carolina Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in North Carolina is $63,339, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in North Carolina

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review North Carolina's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of North Carolina's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in North Carolina?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in North Carolina, the current median is approximately $63,339 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $112,110. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in North Carolina bankruptcy?

North Carolina requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in North Carolina?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

North Dakota Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for North Dakota by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

North Dakota Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for North Dakota by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,749$6,146

2 persons$95,874$7,990

3 persons$112,098$9,342

4 persons$130,536$10,878

5 persons$141,636$11,803

6 people$152,736$12,728

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in North Dakota

Not Available

This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by North Dakota state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

1 district

The single-person median income threshold in North Dakota is $73,749, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Review North Dakota's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

For a single person in North Dakota, the current median is approximately $73,749 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $130,536. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Ohio: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/ohio.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Ohio Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Ohio by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Ohio. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Ohio Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Ohio by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,564$5,297

2 persons$82,633$6,886

3 persons$96,617$8,051

4 persons$112,508$9,376

5 persons$123,608$10,301

6 people$134,708$11,226

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Ohio

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Ohio requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Ohio state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

2 districts

Ohio has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Ohio Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Ohio is $63,564, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Ohio

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Ohio's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Ohio's 2 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Ohio?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Ohio, the current median is approximately $63,564 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $112,508. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Ohio bankruptcy?

Ohio requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Ohio?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

Means Test Guide

What is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

Automatic Stay ·
341 Meeting ·
523(a) Exceptions ·
727(a)(8) ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·
How to File ·
Cost Guide

You May Also Find Helpful

## Chapter 7 Means Test Oklahoma: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/oklahoma.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Oklahoma Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Oklahoma by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Oklahoma. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Oklahoma Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Oklahoma by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$60,636$5,053

2 persons$78,827$6,569

3 persons$92,167$7,681

4 persons$107,326$8,944

5 persons$118,426$9,869

6 people$129,526$10,794

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Oklahoma

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Oklahoma requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Oklahoma state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

Oklahoma has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Oklahoma Income and the Means Test

With a single-person median income of $60,636, Oklahoma has one of the lower income thresholds nationally. Filers whose income is close to this amount should carefully calculate their 6-month average, as even small fluctuations can determine whether they pass Part 1. Timing your filing date strategically can make a difference.

Tips for Filing in Oklahoma

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Oklahoma's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Oklahoma's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Oklahoma?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Oklahoma, the current median is approximately $60,636 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $107,326. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Oklahoma bankruptcy?

Oklahoma requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Oklahoma?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test Oregon: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/oregon.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Oregon Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Oregon by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Oregon. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Oregon Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Oregon by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,347$6,112

2 persons$95,351$7,946

3 persons$111,487$9,291

4 persons$129,824$10,819

5 persons$140,924$11,744

6 people$152,024$12,669

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Oregon

Federal Exemptions

Available

Oregon is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Oregon has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Oregon Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Oregon is $73,347, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Oregon

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Oregon state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Oregon?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Oregon, the current median is approximately $73,347 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $129,824. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Oregon?

Yes. Oregon allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Oregon?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

Means Test Guide

What is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Pennsylvania by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$71,547$5,962

2 persons$93,011$7,751

3 persons$108,751$9,063

4 persons$126,638$10,553

5 persons$137,738$11,478

6 people$148,838$12,403

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Pennsylvania

Federal Exemptions

Available

Pennsylvania is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

Pennsylvania has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Pennsylvania Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Pennsylvania is $71,547, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Pennsylvania

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Pennsylvania state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Confirm which of Pennsylvania's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Pennsylvania?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Pennsylvania, the current median is approximately $71,547 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $126,638. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Pennsylvania?

Yes. Pennsylvania allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Pennsylvania?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Rhode Island Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Rhode Island by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Rhode Island. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Rhode Island Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Rhode Island by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$77,358$6,446

2 persons$100,565$8,380

3 persons$117,584$9,799

4 persons$136,924$11,410

5 persons$148,024$12,335

6 people$159,124$13,260

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Rhode Island

Federal Exemptions

Available

Rhode Island is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

Rhode Island has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

Rhode Island Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Rhode Island is $77,358, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Rhode Island

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Rhode Island state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Rhode Island, the current median is approximately $77,358 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $136,924. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Rhode Island?

Yes. Rhode Island allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Rhode Island?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

1 person$61,050$5,088

2 persons$79,365$6,614

3 persons$92,796$7,733

4 persons$108,058$9,005

5 persons$119,158$9,930

6 people$130,258$10,855

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in South Carolina

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

South Carolina requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by South Carolina state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

South Carolina has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

South Carolina Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in South Carolina is $61,050, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in South Carolina

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review South Carolina's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in South Carolina?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in South Carolina, the current median is approximately $61,050 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $108,058. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in South Carolina bankruptcy?

South Carolina requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in South Carolina?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

## Chapter 7 Means Test South Dakota: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

South Dakota Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for South Dakota by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in South Dakota. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

South Dakota Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for South Dakota by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$68,208$5,684

2 persons$88,670$7,389

3 persons$103,676$8,640

4 persons$120,728$10,061

5 persons$131,828$10,986

6 people$142,928$11,911

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in South Dakota

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

South Dakota requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by South Dakota state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

1 district

South Dakota has one federal bankruptcy district. All bankruptcy cases in the state are filed in this single district court.

South Dakota Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in South Dakota is $68,208, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in South Dakota

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review South Dakota's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in South Dakota?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in South Dakota, the current median is approximately $68,208 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $120,728. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in South Dakota bankruptcy?

South Dakota requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in South Dakota?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Filing Bar Rules

727(a)(8) Eight-Year Filing Bar

Means Test Guide

What is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

341 Meeting ·
523(a) Exceptions ·
727(a)(8) ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·

## Chapter 7 Means Test Tennessee: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/tennessee.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Tennessee Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Tennessee by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Tennessee. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Tennessee Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Tennessee by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$61,602$5,134

2 persons$80,083$6,674

3 persons$93,635$7,803

4 persons$109,036$9,086

5 persons$120,136$10,011

6 people$131,236$10,936

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Tennessee

Federal Exemptions

Not Available

Tennessee requires bankruptcy filers to use state-specific exemptions rather than the federal bankruptcy exemptions. This means the property you can protect in Chapter 7 is determined entirely by Tennessee state law. Understanding your state's homestead exemption, vehicle exemption, and wildcard exemption amounts is critical before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

3 districts

Tennessee has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Tennessee Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Tennessee is $61,602, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Tennessee

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Review Tennessee's exemption schedules carefully -- you must use state exemptions and cannot elect federal exemptions.

Confirm which of Tennessee's 3 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Tennessee?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Tennessee, the current median is approximately $61,602 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $109,036. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What exemptions are available in Tennessee bankruptcy?

Tennessee requires filers to use state exemptions only -- the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption, motor vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption that may apply.

What happens if I fail the means test in Tennessee?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State

Related Guides

## Chapter 7 Means Test Texas: Income Limits and Calculator [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcymeanstest.org/texas.html

Means Test Guide | Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Texas Chapter 7 Means Test: Do You Qualify?

2026 median income limits for Texas by household size. Free calculator and filing guide.

Important: This page provides educational information about the Chapter 7 means test in Texas. Median income data is approximate, based on Census Bureau / DOJ figures effective April 2026. Figures are updated every April and November. Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page. This is not legal advice.

Texas Median Income Thresholds (2026)

The table below shows the approximate annual and monthly income thresholds for Texas by household size. If your annualized income (6-month average multiplied by 12) is at or below the figure for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly Equivalent

1 person$66,837$5,570

2 persons$86,888$7,241

3 persons$101,592$8,466

4 persons$118,301$9,858

5 persons$129,401$10,783

6 people$140,501$11,708

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Try the Means Test Calculator

Enter your income and household size to get an instant result.

Open Calculator

Bankruptcy Filing in Texas

Federal Exemptions

Available

Texas is one of the states that allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions (11 U.S.C. Section 522(d)). This gives filers more flexibility to protect their property. In many cases, the federal exemptions offer higher protection for personal property, while state exemptions may provide better homestead coverage. A careful comparison of both sets is essential before filing.

Bankruptcy Districts

4 districts

Texas has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules and procedures, so check the local rules for your specific district before filing.

Texas Income and the Means Test

The single-person median income threshold in Texas is $66,837, which is near the national middle range. Filers whose income is close to this threshold should carefully average their last 6 calendar months of gross income. If one or two months had unusually high income (bonus, overtime, severance), waiting for those months to fall outside the 6-month window can change the result.

Tips for Filing in Texas

Gather all income documentation for the 6 full calendar months before your planned filing date, including pay stubs, tax returns, 1099s, and bank statements.

Compare both federal and Texas state exemptions to determine which set protects more of your property.

Confirm which of Texas's 4 bankruptcy districts covers your county, and review that district's local rules.

Complete the credit counseling requirement (from an approved provider) before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Texas?

There is no single income limit. The means test compares your annualized income (6-month average times 12) to the median income for your household size. For a single person in Texas, the current median is approximately $66,837 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $118,301. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal bankruptcy exemptions in Texas?

Yes. Texas allows bankruptcy filers to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must choose one complete set -- you cannot mix and match individual exemptions from both. Compare both sets carefully with attention to the homestead, vehicle, and wildcard exemptions.

What happens if I fail the means test in Texas?

Failing Part 1 of the means test (having above-median income) does not disqualify you. You must then complete Part 2 (Form 122A-2), which deducts allowable expenses -- including IRS standard amounts, actual secured debt payments, taxes, insurance, and child care. Many above-median filers pass Part 2. If you still do not pass, Chapter 13 is an alternative that allows debt repayment over 3 to 5 years with no income ceiling.

Explore More Bankruptcy Resources

Free tools and guides from the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Means Test Calculator
Exemptions by State
